title: Pet First Aid Essentials Every Owner Should Know
description: Essential pet first aid tips from our Portland veterinarians. Learn how to handle common pet emergencies before you can reach your vet.
content:
Knowing basic pet first aid can make the difference between a good outcome and a crisis. While first aid is never a substitute for veterinary care, it can stabilize your pet and buy critical time on the way to the clinic.

## Build a Pet First Aid Kit

Keep a dedicated kit at home and a smaller version in your car. Include:

- **Gauze rolls and non-stick pads** for wrapping wounds
- **Medical tape** (the self-adhesive wrap variety works best on fur)
- **Digital thermometer** (normal range: 100.5–102.5°F for dogs and cats)
- **Hydrogen peroxide (3%)** to induce vomiting — but only if directed by a veterinarian
- **Tweezers** for tick or splinter removal
- **Saline solution** for flushing eyes or wounds
- **Your vet's phone number** and the ASPCA Poison Control number (888-426-4435)

## Bleeding

For minor cuts, apply gentle pressure with clean gauze for 5–10 minutes. For deeper wounds or heavy bleeding, apply firm pressure and head to the clinic immediately. Do not remove the gauze if blood soaks through — add more layers on top.

## Choking

If your pet is pawing at their mouth, gagging, or struggling to breathe, look for a visible obstruction. For dogs, you can carefully sweep the mouth with your finger, but be cautious — a panicked pet may bite. If you cannot dislodge the object, perform a modified Heimlich maneuver (sharp upward pressure just behind the rib cage) and get to a vet immediately.

## Heatstroke

Portland summers can bring unexpected heat waves. Signs of heatstroke include excessive panting, drooling, bright red gums, vomiting, and collapse. Move your pet to a cool area, apply room-temperature (not ice-cold) water to their paws and belly, and head to the vet. Heatstroke can cause organ damage rapidly.

## Poisoning

Common household toxins for pets include chocolate, xylitol (found in sugar-free gum), grapes and raisins, lilies (extremely toxic to cats), and rodent bait. If you suspect your pet ingested something toxic, call your vet or the ASPCA Poison Control hotline immediately. Do not induce vomiting unless specifically instructed to do so.

## When to Go to the Emergency Vet

Head to an emergency clinic right away if your pet experiences:

- Difficulty breathing
- Seizures
- Inability to urinate (especially male cats)
- Bloated or distended abdomen
- Uncontrolled bleeding
- Loss of consciousness
- Suspected broken bones

At Forest Heights Veterinary Clinic, we're available Monday through Friday, 8 AM to 6 PM. For after-hours emergencies, visit our emergency page for local emergency hospital contacts and guidance.
